text: CASA-GRANDE, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, on the Rio Gila, in N lat. 33° 40'; so called from an immense ancient edifice, still existing, supposed to have been built by the ancient Mexicans, in their emigration from the coast of California. CASAL, Kasala, or Kazalu, a large village of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Adana, within half-a-mile of the coast, and 12 m. S of Tarsus, of which it forms the port, and to which the road lies through a level well-cultivated country.
CASALANGUIDA, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Citra, in the district and 10 m. SW of Vasto. Pop. 2,306.
CASALBORDINO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo- Citra, 7 m. NW of Vasto. Pop. 1,319.
CASALBORE, a village of Naples, in Principato Ultra, 15 m. ENE from Benevento, on the road to Troja. It is a dreary dilapidated place, on an insulated eminence.
CASALBORGONE, a small town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 12 m. NE of Turin. Pop. 2,000.
CASALBUTTANO, a small town of Lombardy, situated on a navigable canal, 8 m. NW of Cremona. Pop. 2,500.
CASALCRENDI, a village of Malta, 7 m. from Valetta, in the neighbourhood of which some Phœnician remains have recently been discovered.
CASALDUMI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, 30 m. S. of Campobasso. Pop. 3,300.

CASALE, or Casal, an administrative province of the Sardinian territories, in the division of Alexandria. Area 72,000 hectares. Pop. in 1837, 112,000. It produces grain, wine, fruit, lint, and silk. It is divided into 15 mandemento, and 73 comune.—The cap., of the same name, is situated in a plain, 35 m. SW of Milan, 37 m. NE of Turin, on the r. bank of the Po, not far from the site of the ancient Sedula, at an alt. of 76 metres or 249 ft. above sea-level. It is the seat of a bishop, suffragan of Verceil, and of a royal court-of-appeal, a royal college, and an episcopal seminary. The inhabitants, who amount to 16,000, carry on a considerable traffic, particularly in cattle, pigs, and hams.—C. was formerly a place of great strength, and has sustained several sieges, the most memorable of which was in 1629. It was taken by the Imperialists in 1706, by the French in 1745, and during the revolutionary war it frequently changed masters with the rest of Italy.
CASALE, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the Maremma of Volterra, 45 m. S of Pisa. Pop. 817. CAS
CASALE-GUIDI, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Florence, 5 m. SE of Sarravalle. Pop. 2,421.

CASALE-MAGGIORE, a town of Lombardy, in the duchy of Milan, on the l. bank of the Po, 20 m. ESE of Cremona. Pop. 4,300. Glass and earthenware are manufactured here; and there are a few distilleries and tanneries.

CASALE-PUSTERLENGO, or CASAL-PUSTULINGO, a small town of Lombardy, in the duchy of Milan, deleg. of Lodi-et-Crema, 10 m. E of Lodi. Pop. 5,000. The Austrians were driven back from this place to Lodi, by the French, in the memorable campaign of 1796.
CASALGROSSO, a town of Piedmont, situated at the confluence of the small river Meira and the Po, N of Savigliano.
CASALICCHIO, a fishing-port of Naples in Principato-Citra, 9 m. WSW of Vallo. Pop. 1,265.
CASALI-CELICO, a village of Naples, in Calabria Citra, in the jurisdiction of Cosenza.

CASALNOVO, or Casale Nuovo, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 1ma, 14 m. E of Palmi. Pop. 8,241. It commands a noble view of the spacious valley which borders the gulf of Gioia. In 1783 it was devastated by an earthquake.—Also a town in Calabria Citra, 16 m. E of Castrovillari. Pop. 6,000.—Also a town in the prov. and 7 m. NNE of Naples. Pop. 3,120.—Also a village in the prov. of Principato-Citra on an eminence 10 m. from Lago Negro. Pop. 2,298.
CASALPO, a town in the duchy of Parma, 6 m. ENE of Parma.
CASALTRINITA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, 25 m. E of Foggia, on the lagune of Salpi. Pop. 3,785.
CASALVECCHIO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, 15 m. WSW of San Severo. Pop. 1,020, chiefly of Arnaut descent.

CASAMANZA, a river on the W coast of Africa, flowing into the ocean to the N of Cape Roxo, and 16 leagues S of Bald cape. Its origin and course are not yet exactly ascertained; but it is alleged that it inosculates with the Gambia. The French have a settlement at its mouth, on the N bank, and another 10 leagues up the river, on its S bank. The exports from C. chiefly consist of ivory, hides, and dye-woods.
CASAMARCIANO, a town of Naples, in the Terra-de-Lavoro, 1 m. E of Nola. Pop. 1,790. There is a fine convent here, founded in 1134.
CASAMASSIMA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Bari, 12 m. S of Bari. Pop. 4,230.
CASAMICCIOLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Naples, 12 m. SW of Puzzoli, near the foot of Monte-Epomeo. Pop. 3,133. Good wine is made in the district.
CASAN. See KAZAN.

CASANARA, or CAZANARE, a river of South America, in New Granada, in the dep. of Boyaca, which has its rise in the mountain-deserts of Chita, on the easternmost of the three ridges of the Andes, and which flowing in an E course nearly under the parallel of 6° N, through extensive llanos which pasture immense herds of cattle, falls into the Meta, at about 75 leagues from the junction of the latter stream with the great river Orinoco. It is navigable for sloops throughout the year; in the dry season, the navigation is assisted by continual and fresh breezes; while in winter the vessels, which keep nearer the shore, are carried down by the strength of the current, with equal certainty though more slowly. By means of this river, and of the river Meta, together with their various tributary streams, the inhabitants of Santa-Fé were enabled to carry their produce into Guayana, and from thence to Europe; and this beneficial intercourse had already produced a sensible improvement on the cultivation of the countries which bordered on these two rivers, when it was stopped on the representation of the merchants of Cartagena, who claimed an exclusive right to the commerce of Santa-Fé. The inhabitants of Santa-Fé were in consequence only allowed to send by this channel into Guayana, cotton, flour, and other bulky articles.-The small town of C. on the r. bank of the river, is 60 m. SW of Arauca in Venezuela
CASANDRINO, a large village of Naples, in the district of Casoria, 6 m. N of Naples, with 2,100 inhabitants.
CASANOVA, a town of Naples, in the Terra-di- Lavoro, 2 m. WNW of Caserta. Pop. 2,800.
CASAPA, a town of Paraguay, 10 m. SE of Assumption.

CASAPALCA, a village of Peru, on the descent from the pass of Anticona, in the cordillera of Tucto. Barley cannot be grown here; but the lama thrives on the pasture within a short distance of the snow-line.—Smith.
CASAPULLA, a town of Naples, in the Terradi-Lavoro, 4 m. WNW of Caserta. Pop. 2,868.

CASARABONELA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 25 m. W of Malaga. It is a clean and well-paved town, embosomed in groves of fruit-trees and vineyards; and, being situated on the side of a mountain, commands a fine view of the vale of Malaga. Pop. 4,666.
CASARANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Otranto, 12 m. ESE of Gallipoli. Pop. 2,748.
CASAR-DE-CACERES, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, 6 m. NNW of Caceres.

CASARES, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 55 m. WSW of Malaga, 7 m. SSW of Gaucin, on the summit of a narrow ridge rising above the valley of the Genal, and connected with the Sierra Cristellina. Pop. 4,500. The principal part of the town is clustered round the base of an old fortress, the houses rising one above another in steps. The streets, "barely wide enough to allow a paniered donkey to pass freely, are formed out of the live rock, and here and there are cut in wide steps to render the ascent less difficult." This town was the Cæsaris Salutariensis of Pliny, so designated from the mineral waters in its neighbourhood.—Scott.
CASARRUBIOS-DEL-MONTE, or CESARUBIOS, a small town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 20 m. N of Toledo. Pop. 1,000.
CASAS-DE-DON-PEDRO, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 65 m. E of Badajoz, on the r. bank of the Tagus.
CASAS-DEL-CONDE, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 30 m. SSW of Salamanca, on the Rio-Francia.
CASATISMA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 4 m. NE of Voghera.

CASAVATORE, a town of Naples, in the prov. and 4 m. N of Naples. Pop. 1,397. CASBIN, CASVIN, or KAZVIN, a city of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, situated on a great sandy plain, 9 m. W of the highest branch of Mount Taurus, in N lat. 36° 12′, E long. 49° 33′. It is one of the most extensive cities in the kingdom, though a large portion of it is now in ruins. The city is of a square form, each side about 1 m. long, and surrounded by a wall. Most of the houses, which consist of sundried bricks cemented by lime, are sunk deep into the ground, and are flat-roofed. Nadir Shah built a palace in C. adjoining to an old one wholly or partly erected by Shah Abbas the Great. The manufactures and trade of C. are considerable, this city being CAS open to Georgia, Azerbijan, Ghilan, and the Caspian sea. Carpets made here, of pieces of cloth of different colours, are much valued, and bear a high price; velvets, brocades, and sword-blades are also manufactured. Morier was informed in 1812, that the adult male pop. of this city then numbered 25,000.

CASCA, a small river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, which rises in the Serra dos Arripiados, runs N, receives the Coroados, passes the village of the same name, and unites with the Doce, below the confluence of the Chopoto.—Also an auriferous stream in the prov. of Goyaz, a tributary of the Vermelho.
CASCABEL (EL), a rocky islet in the Spanish main, 65 m. SW of Jamaica, in N lat. 17° 30'.

CASCA-D'ANTA (CACHOEIRA DA), a cataract of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, at the source of the San Francisco. It has a descent of 667 ft., and terminates in a fine semicircular basin, bordered with rugged fragments of rock.

CASCADE BAY, a bay and port in the straits of Magellan, on the N coast of Clarence island, so named by Bougainville from a remarkable waterfall in the interior of the port. It has deep water, and is surrounded by high land. Sugar-loaf hill, in the vicinity, is in S lat. 53° 57′ 32", W long. 71° 24′ 13".

CASCADE MOUNTAINS, a lofty range of mountains in Oregon, which, at an average distance from the sea-coast of about 130 m., extend both N and S of the Columbia river, joining on the S the Sierra Nevada range of Alta California, and connected on the N with the mountainous region N of Frazer's river. It obtains its name from the difficulties it opposes to the passage of the Colombia river to the Pacific, which breaks its way through it in a series of rapids and falls, between the parallels of 45° 30' and 45° 50'. This range is indicated to the distant observer, both in course and position, by the lofty volcanic peaks which rise out of it, and which are visible at an immense distance. Among these, the most beautiful in form, if not the highest, is Mount St. Helen's, the Mount Washington of the Americans, which attains an alt. of 17,000 ft. This volcano was in action in 1841, and had scattered its ashes over the Dalles of the Columbia, 50 m. distant. The C. range forms a distinct boundary between the climates of the regions along its W and E bases. On the W it presents a barrier to the clouds which roll up from the Pacific, and breaking on its rugged sides, form the rainy season of the winter in the country along the coast; but this rain never penetrates into the region stretching along the E base of the range, and here the winter is comparatively brief and mild.
CASCADE POINT, a cape on the W coast of the middle island of New Zealand, in S lat. 44° 5'.
CASCAES, a small seaport of Portugal, in the well-known district of Torres Vedras, on the N side of the Tagus, at its mouth, 15 m. W of Lisbon. Pop. 3,000.
CASCAES (CAPE), a promontory on the W coast of Portugal, 2 m. SW of the town of Cascaes, in N lat. 38° 42'.
CASCAJAL, an islet off the coast of New Granada, 6 m. E of Point-Galera, and 2 m. from the mainland.
CASCANTE, a small town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, 45 m. NW of Saragossa, 4 m. S of Tudela, on the Queyles. Pop. 2,000. It has distilleries of brandy, and saltpetre works.
CASCASTEL, a town of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Durban, on the Berre. Pop. 698. Lead and copper are mined in the neighbourhood.
CASCAVEL, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of ~ Ceara, at the foot of the mountain from which it | takes its name, and 20 m. SE of Aquiras, on the Choro. Pop. 8,690, chiefly agricultural.
CASCHAU. See KASCHAU.
CASCIA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, deleg. and 15 m. E of Spoleto. Pop. 3,200.
CASCIANA, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. of Pisa. Pop. 1,027. Fine marbles are quarried in the vicinity.
CASCIANO (San), a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 10 m. S of Florence. Pop. 2,000.
CASCIANO-DE-BAGNI (SAN), a parish and bitants. town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 40 m. SE of Sienna, on Monte-Cetona, at an alt. of 1,656 ft. There are hot sulphurous baths here.
CASCINA, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 10 m. SE of Pisa, near the l. bank of the Arno. Pop. 2,244.

CASCO BAY, a bay of the state of Maine, U.S., which spreads NW between Cape Elizabeth on the SW, and Cape Small Point on the NE. Within these points, which are about 20 m. apart, there are nearly 300 small islands, some of which are inhabited, and nearly all more or less cultivated. Several smaller bays are included within the limits of C. bay. Various rivers pour their streams into it; and several arms or creeks of salt water extend from it into the country, to the great benefit of its internal commerce and navigation.
CASCOB, a parish, partly in Herefordshire and Radnorshire. Pop. in 1841, 171.—Also a township, partly in the parishes of Cascob and Presteigne, Herefordshire. Pop., with the township of Litton, 102.
CASCUMPEQUE, a small fishing-village and harbour in Prince Edward's island, 16 m. N of Richmond bay.

CASEDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, 21 m. ESE of Pamplona, partido and 10 m. S of Sanguesa, on a rising ground, on the Aragon, which is here crossed by a handsome bridge. Pop. 1,272. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains several churches, an hospital, and mineral springs and baths.
CASEI, or Case, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento, in the prov. and 5 m. W of Voghera, on the l. bank of the Curone. Pop. 1,500.
CASE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Monbazens, near Peyrusse. It contains a mine of argentiferous lead.
CASELLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Principato Citra, district and 17 m. S of La Sala, and 10 m. N of Policastro. It has an annual fair.

CASELLE, or Cassel, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandimento, in the prov. and 8 m. NNW of Turin, on an arm of the Stura. Pop. 3,500. It contains several churches, a convent, and an hospital, and has manufactories of cloth and other woollen fabrics, calico, silk-spinning, paper and fulling-mills. -Also a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation of Lodi-e-Crema, 8 m. WSW of Lodi, district and 4 m. NW of S. Angelo. Pop. 1,700.
CASELLE-LANDI, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation of Lodi-e- Crema, district and 7 m. SE of Codogno, and 22 m. SE of Lodi, near the l. bank of the Po. Pop. 1,500.
CASELLINA, a parish of Tuscany, 5 m. W of Florence. Pop, 1,067. It possesses extensive manufactories of straw-bonnets.
CASE MARTINELLI, a town of the States-ofthe-Church, in the delegation and 25 m. SE of Forli, and 5 m. NW of Rimini.
CASE NAVIRE, a town of the island of Martinique, on the W coast, at the mouth of a river of the same name, and 2 m. NW of Fort Royal. CAS

CASE-PILOTE (LA), a parish and town of the island of Martinique, on the W coast, 5 m. NW of Fort Royal. The soil possesses little fertility, but the sugar cane is cultivated with success. Pop. 2,250, of whom 218 are whites, 1,821 slaves, and 211 freecoloured.

CASENTINO, a district of Tuscany, in the comp. of Florence, comprising the highest of the basins of the Arno, and extending from the source of that river to Dicomano. It contains an area of 286 Italian sq. m., and possessed, in 1834, 34,536 inha-

CASERTA-NOVA, a town of Naples, the cap. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, 15 m. NNE of Naples, in the middle of a fertile plain. Pop. 19,438. It is an irregularly built town, but has several good churches, and superb barracks, and a palace of vast extent, "an immense quadrangle full of windows," [Simond], founded in 1752. A manufactory of rich silk-stuffs exists here.
CASERTA-VECCHIA, a town of Naples, 2 m. NE of the above. It is the see of a bishop, and has a fine cathedral.

CASEY, a county in the S part of the state of Kentucky, U. S., drained by the head-waters of the Greene. Area 448 sq. m. The cap. is Liberty. Pop. 4,939.—Also a township in Clark co. in Illinois, 121 m. ESE of Springfield.
CASHCARRIGAN, or Keshcarrigan, a village in the p. of Kiltubrid, co. Leitrim, 6 m. NE of Carrick-on-Shannon. Pop. 132.

CASHEEN, a bay in co. Galway, on the W side of the island of Garomna, 83 m. due N of the most westerly point of the Arran islands. It is completely covered, at a distance of less than half-a-mile, by the islands of Lettermullin and Furnis, has excellent shelter, and is easy of access.

CASHEEN, a river of co. Kerry, formed by the confluence of the Feale, the Gale, and the Brick, at a point 24 m. NNE of Lixnaw. It runs northward to the Shannon.—The great bog-district of C., within the basin of the above river, is bounded on the north by the Shannon; on the E and S by the argillaceous uplands of the county; and on the W by the ocean. Its area in morass is 31,514 acres.

CASHEL, a village in co. Sligo, 6 m. S of Sligo.— Also a parish 4 m. S of Lanesborough, in co. Longford. Area 22,150 acres. Pop. in 1831, 5,087; in 1841, 5,559. Houses 929. The surface extends between an expanse of morass on the E, and Lough Ree on the W. The principal small lakes are Shawn and Derrymacar in the interior, and Derry on the E boundary, lying as to surface-elevation 127 ft. above sea-level.

CASHEL, a post and market-town, a borough, an episcopal city, and the ecclesiastical metropolis of the S province of Ireland, co. Tipperary. It stands on the road from Dublin to Cork by way of Athy and Fermoy, 34 m. NE of Golden, 47 m. NNE of Cork, and 79 m. SW by S of Dublin. The Municipal reports, though they do not specifically name parochial distribution, state the pop. of 'the city,' in 1831, at 6,971, and that of "Cashel, including the liberties and commons," at near 12,000. The country around C. is an extensive plain,—the broad, flattened, fertile valley of the Suir, which flows at the distance of 24 m. on the W. The far-famed 'Rock of C.' shoots up from the N edge of the town, and arrests the eve from almost every spot of ground over a great extent of circumjacent country; but the town itself lies so flatly prostrate at the rock's base, and on a low part of the prevailing level, as nowhere to be seen from any of the great thoroughfares till it is about to be entered. C. appears, when just about to be entered. to possess many features of beauty; and yet it presents a large aggregate of meanness, poverty, and filth. Of 1,135 houses which lined all the streets and alleys in 1836, only 190 were individually worth £10 and upwards a-year, while as many as 751 were thatched tenements, and by far the greater part of these one-story cottages, ill-built cabins, or miserable huts and hovels. The water-works, constructed by Archbishop Bolton, during the early part of the 18th cent., have been allowed to go to decay, and their under-ground conduits, upwards of 2 m. in length, are choked up with rubbish. The most interesting ruin on the Rock, not alone for its high antiquity, but for the gracefulness of its proportions and the high finish of its workmanship, is Cormac's chapel.—The cathedral consists of a choir, a nave, transepts, and a square tower rising from the centre of the cruciform intersection. It measures about 210 ft. from E to W, and about 170 ft. along the transepts. In 1647, after having been fortified for the purposes of the civil war, it was stormed and captured by Lord Inchiquin; between 1744 and 1752, it was unroofed by Archbishop Price; and, a short time afterwards, Archbishop Agar made a vain attempt to restore it, and felt compelled to supplant it by the erection of the new cathedral.—Hore abbey stands on the plain 550 yards W, in a straight line, from the summit of the Rock.—The modern cathedral and parish-church of C. is situated in an open area, surrounded by streets, 700 yards S of the summit of the Rock. It was erected about the middle of the last century; and is an elegant structure, surmounted by a plain but lofty spire.—The archiepiscopal palace is a spacious and plain yet respectable mansion, surrounded with fine gardens which extend to the Rock's base. Under the new hierarchical arrangement, which destroys the archiepiscopate of C., and unites all the four sees of Cashel, Emly, Waterford, and Lismore, C. palace will be abandoned, and the palace of Waterford will be the residence of the diocesan. A public library, attached to the palace, contains some valuable manuscripts.—The new Roman Catholic chapel is a spacious edifice, and occupies most of the site of the Franciscan abbey.—A small barrack for infantry contains accommodation for two companies.—The court-houses are a good feature in the town's architecture.—The town has no considerable manufacture. Agriculture affords the main employment to the working-classes. The retail supply of general goods to the surrounding country, and a very limited sale of agricultural and pastoral produce at the weekly markets and annual fairs, constitute by far the greater part of the trade. The Cork line of railway, as projected by the Railway commissioners, touches the town at 98 miles' distance from Dublin, and if executed would bring it within 4 hours' travelling of the metropolis. The limits of the modern borough comprehend the whole of the modern town, and only so much of unedificed ground as lies between the streets of the town's extremities; and the space which they include measures about 1,200 by about 900 yards. The constituency registrations under the Reform Act amounted in 1840-1 to 289. The city sends one member to parliament. The Diocese. The see of C. claims to have been founded at the beginning of the 10th cent., and to have been erected into an archbishopric in 1152. The dio. includes small pendicles on the W border of the co. of Kilkenny and the E border of the co. of Limerick; but, with the exception of these, it all lies in the co. of Tipperary, comprehends most of the central division, and upwards of half of the southern division of that county. The Ecclesiastical commissioners state its length and breadth at respec- * CAS pop. in 1831, at 181,148; and its parishes at 90, besides one chapelry.—The gross Episcopal revenues of this see and that of Emly are inseparably mixed, and amount to £6,861 15s. 10d.; and the net revenues to £6,308 5s. 2d. The dignitaries of the diocese are dean, precentor, chancellor, treasurer, archdeacon, and 5 prebendaries. History.] C. is chiefly famous in history as the scene, in 1172, of a congress of nobles, at which Donald O'Brian and the heads of Irish septs did homage to Henry II.; and of a synod of the Irish clergy, presided over by the pope's legate, at which the Irish archbishops and bishops gave to the king sealed charters, afterwards confirmed by Pope Alexander, conferring for ever the kingdom of Ireland on Henry II. and his heirs. During the long and cruel contests between the Butlers and Fitzgeralds, the city was a frequent sufferer. In 1647, the Lord Inchiquin, at the head of the Parliamentary forces, marched against C. The citizens retired to the Rock, as both a citadel and a sanctuary, and refused the offer of Inchiquin to leave them unmolested upon payment of £3,000 to his army. The result was, that the fortress was taken by storm; many of the inhabitants, including 20 monks, were slain; and the city and its people were given up to plunder

CASHGAR. See KASHGAR. CASHMERE, CASHMEER, or KASHMIR, a district of Northern Hindostan, formerly subject to the sovereign of Candahar, and recently to the Sikh maharajah of Lahore. It is an Alpine valley, or rather basin, of an elliptical form, surrounded on all sides by lofty snow-clad mountains, and intersected by numerous ridges and gorges connected with the limiting ranges, and all having a lofty pass at their upper extremity. In its utmost extent, it lies between the parallels of 33° 15' and 34° 30' N, and the meridians of 73° 45' and 75° 40'; but, excluding the declivities of the mountain-cincture, the plain forming the bottom of the basin does not exceed 85 m. in length from WNW to ESE; and is from 30 m. to only 6 m. in breadth. Its superficies is variously estimated at from 4,500 to 5,000 sq. m. Its average alt. above sea-level is estimated by Vigne at 5,000 ft.; by Hugel at 5,818 ft. Boundaries. The enclosing mountain-range bears the general name of the Panjals, and forms, according to Hugel, "a regular oval of snowy summits;" but, with the exception of one summit, in N lat. 34° 10', E long. 75° 5', the estimated alt. of which is 19,650 ft., the elevation of the Panjals is greatly inferior to that of the Hindukoosh, or great central chain of the Himalaya. Beginning our survey of the mountain-cincture of C. at the great basaltic mountain of Haramuk, in N lat. 34° 25', E long. 74° 40' [alt. 13,400 ft.?], to the S of the great pass from the Walur lake, or from Sirinagur, to Iskardoh in Little Tibet, we trace the extreme mountain-boundary running in an ESE direction to the Bultul pass, in N lat. 34° 10′, E long. 75° 15′ [alt. 10,500 ft.], near the source of the Duras river, which flows NE to join the Indus, and of a small river flowing SW, a mountain-feeder of the Sinde river which belongs to the basin of the Jelum. From this point, the mountainboundary is prolonged eastwards on the N side of the Sinde river, to its source, bending round which, and around the source of the Lidur, it attains, near the Shesha-Nag, in N lat. 34° 7', E long. 75° 35' [alt. 13,000 ft.], its extreme E point; and from this latter point to Ramhur, the extreme SE point of C., it runs in a direction a little to the W of S, in a continuous ridge, forming the water-shed between the basin of the Chenab on the E, and that of the C. valley on the W. In this portion of the cincture there occurs only one pass, leading from the valley of the Gohun, in the S part of C., to Pambur. From Ramhur, the Panjals, under the names of the Panjal of Banihal and Futi- Panjal, run N of W to a pass in the SW corner of C., which crosses the basaltic range of the Futi-Panjal, under the meridian of 74° 33', at an alt. of 11,800 ft. tively 35 and 29 m.; its area, at 418,815 acres; its On this line of southern frontier, the principal pass is the Banihal pass, leading from Harrawug on the Chenab, N to Shahbad, and which has its summit [10,807 ft.] in N lat. 33° 25', E long. 75° 10'. To the W of this pass is the Col-Narwah, or Kol-Naruwa, alt. 12,500 ft.; the Didyum, alt. 13,000 ft.; and the Kosa-Nag, at the source of the Veshau river, a head-tributary of the Jelum, which Vigne estimates at 12,000 ft., and Walker at 13,500 ft. From the W extremity of the Futi-Panjal, the mountain-boundary, to the parallel of 34° 25' N, has a NNW course, and on its highest portion, known as the Pir-Panjal, forming the SW side of the valley of C., attains an elevation of 15,000 ft., according to Hugel. The Pir- Panjal pass, in N lat. 33° 30', E long. 74° 25', leading from Rajawur on the Tohi river, across the mountains and into the valley of the Damdam river, has an alt. of 12,952 ft. according to Hugel, of 11,800 ft. according to Vigne, and 11,970 ft. according to Jacquemont. Under the parallel of 34°, occurs the pass of Ferozepur; and under that of 34° 10', the great Baramula pass, through which the Jelum finds an outlet from the valley of C. into the Punjab. The remaining portion of the mountain-cincture of C., forming its N frontier, sweeps round in a semicircular form to the Haramuk, the point from which our rapid outline of the mountain-boundary of C. commenced. In general terms, C. has on the N, Little Tibet; on the E, Ladak; on the S and W, the small Sikh states of Chumba, Jummu, and Rajawur or Rajur, in Upper Lahore. General features.] The valley of C., whose boundaries we have now traced, is celebrated for its romantic beauties, the fertility of its soil, the temperature of its atmosphere, and the picturesque variety of its landscapes. The author of the Ayeen Acberry dwells with rapture on the beauties of this district; and the ideas which have been entertained of it may in some degree be understood from the epithets by which it has been so commonly characterised, - 'the happy valley,'-'the garden in perpetual spring,'-'the paradise of India.' A recent traveller, Baron Hugel, says: "The whole valley is like a nobleman's park; the villages being surrounded with fruit-trees; and, having in their centre immense plane and poplar trees, form large masses, having between them one sheet of cultivation, through which the noble river winds itself in elegant sweeps." The periodical rains which almost deluge the rest of India, are shut out of C. by the height of the mountains, so that only light showers fall here; but these are in sufficient abundance to feed thousands of cascades, which are precipitated into the valley from every part of the stupendous and romantic bulwark by which this favoured spot is encircled. The soil of the plains is everywhere rich. It is conceived to have been composed of the mud deposited by the principal river of the district, which flowing in this direction, and finding no outlet, at first formed its waters into a vast lake that covered the whole valley to a depth of 300 or 400 ft., but eventually having opened for itself a passage through the mountains, left this spot an ample field for human industry. Although this account, says Major Rennel, has no living testimony to support it, yet history and tradition, and-what is yet stronger-appearances, have impressed a conviction of its truth on the minds of all who have visited the scene, and contemplated the different parts of it. Such indeed, he remarks, must be the economy of nature in all cases, when water is enclosed in any part of its course by elevated lands. If the lake be formed near the sources of the river, and the ground is solid, it may remain for an indefinite length of time, or for ever, in the same state, for want of sufficient force in the river to work out a passage for itself; but when a lake has been formed in the lower part of a large river, even the most apparently insuperable obstacles will in such circumstances be forced at last to give way. In the same manner the Euphrates has been found to open a passage for itself through Mount Taurus, and the Ganges through the Imaus. The length of time which the lake in C. seems to have occupied in effecting its emancipation, is evinced by the great depth of soil deposited by it previously to its departure. From various data and observations, Vigne infers that the basaltic ridges around C. have been raised from beneath, and through extensive beds of limestone in the deep ocean; that the mountains near Shenkur-Gurh, in the Baramula pass, which would otherwise have opposed the exit of the waters altogether in that quarter, were rent asunder at the same time; but that the lower accidental barriers, such as that immediately below Baramula, at Uri, and below Nushera, although likewise, and particularly the latter, partially divided more or less by convulsions, were yet sufficiently elevated to oppose the free passage of a great part of the alluvium carried by the flood in its course down the pass; and consequently, the pass was-as it evidently has beenchoked up with deposit, over and through which the waters from the newly formed lake have gradually formed a deeper channel for themselves. Further, that in consequence of the occasional barriers of hard rock, a succession of pools and head-waters, such as at Nushera, have formed above them; that these have disappeared as the bed of the river became more uniformly level; that below the gap at Uri, where the action of the river is observable on the perpendicular walls of the rock for a height of 50 or 60 ft. above it, the cross action of the Punch-Panjal river has very materially contributed suddenly to deepen the bed of the Jelum, as it flows through the amphitheatre in the mountains immediately below the gap; and that as this must have taken place beneath the western face of the solid rock, it is probable that the Jelum once poured its waters over the ledge, in a cataract of great magnificence. The sudden breaking-up and carrying away of any part of the obstacles in the pass may, of course, have caused sudden subsidencies in the level of the lake. But, Vigne adds, "I believe that the great proportion of the shingly conglomerate, which must have been originally included within the vale of C., and of which, in places protected from the wear and tear of the water, I have shown that there is some still remaining, must have been broken up gradually, and carried away by the river, or mixed with and sunk in the soil produced by the detritus from either side of the valley. The enormous quantity of soil still remaining in the valley, when there is, comparatively speaking, so little in the neighbouring countries, is owing to its escape having been so much prevented by the filling up of the Baramula pass. The flat surfaces of the karywas [elevated plains in the centre of the valley], whose cliffs are 150 to 200 ft. above the lowest part of the valley, is attributable to their having for ages remained at the bottom of a still lake, perhaps at least 300 ft. above its present level at the bottom of that valley. If I may judge by the moveable elevation of the first made barrier at Baramula, their existence is partly owing to their being no longer acted upon by the streams that have reduced them to their present size and shape; and partly to their being protected-as are those of Islamabad and Pampur-by a nucleus of solid rock. It is, I think, quite evident, that the waters which once fed the lake of the valley are now pouring themselves into the bed of the Jylum, which has gradually worn itself a channel in the lowest part of it; and that the reason why the karywas on either extremity of the broad belt of meadow-land on its banks, in the upper part of its course, are suddenly * terminated in a succession of cliffs, is because the highest floods of the river after it had once settled itself in its present channel, have not been able to extend their ravages laterally beyond the point marked by the line of their bases. For the foregoing reasons, I fully believe in the tradition of the natives as to the valley having been originally a lake of between 300 or 400 ft. in depth. But I also believe its drainage to have been the work of time; and I do not think there is a foundation for that part of the tradition which would refer its dessication to the effect of a sudden and subsequent formation of an outlet through the Baramula pass." [Travels, vol. ii., pp. 283-287.] Rivers.] The Jelum river is, even within the limits of C., a large river, and navigable at least for small craft. Its current, throughout the whole valley, from SE to NW, is remarkably smooth, though the body of water contained in it is very great. Its breadth is irregular; but in some places it is not less than 2 m. In one part of its course, it is formed, by the hollow surface of the country, into a sheet of water, 21 m. long, and 9 m. wide, known by the name of the Ouller, Wulur, or Great lake. The outlet through which it departs from the valley, and where it runs with much greater rapidity and force than elsewhere, is at Barchmooleh, or Baramula, between two steep mountains, where the walls of the valley appear, except at the cleft itself, to be "as unbroken and undivided as in every other part of the horizon," [Vigne], and whence proceeding, after a long course, it joins with the Chenab. In this river, now known by the name of the Behut, Chelum, Jelum, Jelam, or Jailum, we recognise the famous Hydaspes of antiquity, which Alexander the Great crossed, about 100 m. below the limits of the valley. The principal tributaries of the Jelum are the Breng, the Lidur, the Veshau, the Rembeara, and the Ramu, all of which unite their contributions to it to the S of the parallel of 34°. In the vicinity of Sirinagur, the dal, or City lake, a sheet of water 6 m. long, and 4 m. broad, discharges itself into the Jelum, on the r. bank, by a channel about 2 m. in length; and between Sirinagur and the Wulur lake, it receives a large tributary, the Sinde, rising on the extreme E point of C. Between its exit from the Wulur lake, and the Baramula pass, where its course takes a W direction—its principal tributary, the Pohru, composed of the Lolab, the Kanimil, and the river of Budrikul, by which it drains the N part of the valley, join it on the r. bank. Climate.] Surrounded by mountains whose summits are covered with snow during the greater part of the year, and elevated 5,000 ft. above sea-level, the climate of C. in winter is very severe; but the Jelum is seldom completely frozen over. Frosts set in about the middle of November, and snow lies to the average depth of 2 ft. from December to April. April and May are, as with us, months of intermingled showers and sunshine. The greatest heat occurs from the middle of July to the middle of August, when the therm. ranges in the shade from 80° to 85°. Productions.] As the surface of this district is generally flat and copiously watered, it yields abundant crops of rice which is the common food of the inhabitants. The returns are from 30 to 60 fold. At the base of the hills are cultivated wheat, barley, millet, maize, and various other descriptions of grain and of pulse. Hemp and saffron are extensively grown, likewise mustard, flax, and a great variety of esculent vegetables. The grape is not wellmanaged; but a wine is made in this country resembling Madeira; and a spirituous liquor is distilled from the grape. The chunar or plane-tree—Platanus orientalis—common in most parts of Asia, is said to arrive at greater perfection here than in other coun- CASHMERE. tries. It has, when in full foliage, a grand and beautiful appearance, and in hot weather affords a refreshing shade. By order of the Mogul emperors, this tree and the poplar were planted in every village; but it is said that these trees are exhibiting symptoms of decay. The lime and the chestnut are also common; and the birch and alder, with junipers and rhododendrons, clothe the higher parts of the mountains up to the limit of perpetual snow. Roses grow in vast profusion; and the syringa, jasznine, and Daphne, are common. The fruits of C. are pronounced by Hugel to be the finest of their species. Those which attain maturity are the apple, pear, peach, apricot, plum, almond, pomegranate, mulberry, walnut, pistachio-nut, grape, and melon. The singhara, or water-nut—Trapa bispinosa—grows in immense profusion on the bottom of the Wulur lake, and is said to be annually gathered to the extent of 60,000 tons. These nuts are eaten raw, boiled, or roasted; and though insipid in taste, are highly nutritious, and afford a main if not exclusive article of subsistence to 20,000 persons. The Egyptian water-lily- Nymphæa lotus—is common in the lakes and ponds. The zoology of C. is highly varied. The brown and the black bear, and the wolf, occur in the mountain-forests. The jackal, fox, otter, ichneumon, and a species of small panther, are also found here. Stags, deer, gazelles, and goats, are numerous and of various species. Among the birds of C. are the vulture, eagle, heron, crane, jay, hoopoo, and bulbul. The latter is a distinct species of nightingale from that of Europe, and greatly inferior to it in song.— Venomous serpents are rare.—Bees are very numerous; and silk-worms are reared. Of the domestic animals, the horses of C. are a small but hardy breed, and asses and mules are numerous. The native sheep are of a small breed; that with a broad tail, called dumba, has become extinct. Goats are numerous. There seems no reason to doubt that the finest breeds of horses and cattle might be reared upon the extensive mountain-pastures of this region, where every variety of temperature can be commanded for them. Manufactures and commerce. The principal articles of native manufacture are woollen shawls, gun and pistol barrels, leather, paper, lacquered ware and attar of roses.—The celebrated shawls known by the name of Cashmeres, though manufactured in this country, are not a native product: the longhaired goat, of the hair or duvet of which they are chiefly made, being confined to the mountainous part of Tibet. Amritsir is the great entrepot of this commerce; and the number of shawls annually exported at present is about 100,000; a rival manufactory, however, has been set agoing in Delhi. The raw material, —which is a fine down, growing close to the skin under the upper fleece of the goat, and is also found on the yak, and wild sheep, -is purchased at Kilghet in Ladakh, and also in the vicinity of Yarkand. The finest kind is of a white colour, and is sold in C. at the rate of about 4s. per lb. The finer part of this wool, after having had the long hairs picked out of it, and having been carefully washed in riceflour water, is hand-spun by women. Three weavers are employed on an embroidered shawl of an ordinary pattern for about three months; but some shawls will occupy them 18 months. They are dyed in the yarn, and carefully washed after being woven. The embroidered border is generally separately made, and joined by sewing to the middle part. A pair of fine shawls cost, in the labour of 24 artisans for 12 months, £80; materials and dyeing, £30; duty, £70; minor charges, £20. Total, £200. In the time of the Mogul emperors C. found work for 30,000 shawl-looms; but under Sikh oppression, this important manufacture greatly deelined. The average value of shawls annually exported from C. under Runjeet Singh's rule, was 18,00,000 rupees. Runjeet took two-thirds in kind as part of the gross revenue of the prov.; sold threefourths of what he thus received, and kept the remainder for his court. Of the rest left for sale in the valley, or disposed of by him, 7 lacs worth went to Bombay and Western India; 3 lacs to Hindostan, chiefly Oude; and half-a-lac each was taken by Calcutta, Cabul, Herat, and Balkh .- The gun and pistol barrels manufactured in C. are likewise of great beauty and highly esteemed throughout India. The iron forges in C. produce about 30 kirwahs = 5,760 lbs. in a month; and about two-thirds of this are worked up in C.; but the best iron ore is brought from the Eusufzee country and from Ladak.—The paper of C., when made from the filaments of the wild hemp, is of great excellence, and brings from 5s. to 6s. per quire of 24 sheets. -The celebrated perfume, known as the attar of roses, is distilled from the Rosa biflora. Between 5 and 6 cwt. of leaves are required to produce one ounce of the attar.—Hugel estimates the exports of C. at £400,000; of which £250,000 are in woollen articles; £100,000 in rice; and £50,000 in miscellaneous articles. Population. The pop. of C. is estimated by Mooreroft in 1819, at 800,000, and by Hamilton at 600,000. But Hugel is of opinion that, in 1835, it did not exceed 200,000. A famine, brought on by frost occurring at the time the rice was in flower, and cholera following in consequence of that, had reduced the pop. to this small number. — The Cashmerians are stout and well-formed; and, as natives of a country that lies so much within the range of the greater heats of the sun, may be considered a fair complexioned people. They have been praised, but apparently without adequate ground, for the fineness of their features, and for countenances rather of European than Tartarian aspect. It would appear, however, that the features even of their females are broad, and that there prevails among them a coarseness of figure, though many of the females are very handsome. Their dress consists of a large turban, a great woollen vest with wide sleeves, under which people of the higher class wear a pirahun, or shirt, and drawers -and a sash wrapped in numerous folds round the middle. The women of the higher class are seldom seen abroad. Their dress is a red gown, with large loose sleeves, and a red fillet on the forehead over which is thrown a white mantilla. The hair, collected in separate plaits, is gathered together; and a long tassel of black cotton, suspended from it, reaches almost down to the ancles. There is no purdah, or concealment of the features, except amongst the higher classes. The latter classes have the complexion of brunettes, with more pink on the cheek. —The language of C. is, by some writers, considered to be peculiar, and of very ancient date; others hold that it is derived from the Sanscrit. It is written in the Devanagari character, and resembles in sound that of the Mahrattas, but with more harshness; on which account, probably, the inhabitants of this country have been accustomed to compose their songs in the Persic language, or have adopted those of the Persian poets. Notwithstanding the rugged character of their speech, it is said that a taste for music is universal amongst all classes of the people of this province.—The present pop. consists of Mussulmans and Hindus—the former bearing a very large proportion to the latter. The ancient religion or lake, afford to the inhabitants pleasant retreats. II. $ CASHMERE. of this country, in common with the rest of Central Asia, was Hindu; though it appears to have been of a less severe cast in C. than in the neighbouring provinces. Mr. Vigne was informed that a sati, or suttee, had never taken place in C. until the time of the Sikhs,—that is, was unknown while it was in possession of its successive Mahommedan masters who forbade the custom. Divisions, &c.] C. is divided into 36 pergunnahs; and contains 10 towns, and upwards of 2,000 villages. The principal cities are Sirinagur or Cashmere, the eapital, Islamabad, and Shabad. - Moorcroft states that the revenue of C. as collected by the Sikhs in 1818 was 36 lacs of rupees per ann., of which 12 lacs were raised from the land rent, grain, and saffron, and 24 lacs from duties on shawls and merchandise. He adds that, at that period, not oneeighteenth part of the arable land was under cultivation; and that under proper government, the landrent alone would yield 50 lacs per ann. In 1835, C. returned almost no revenue to Runjeet Singh; and in 1836, according to Baron Hugel, it could not raise his demand of 23 lacs. History.] The ancient Hindu dynasty was extinguished in C. at an early period. In 1323, it was ravaged by the Tartars, whose princes held sway here from that period till 1541, when it was conquered by Mirza Hayder, the general of the emperor Humayum. Akbar annexed this country to Hindostan in 1586; and the Moguls of Delhi ruled here till 1754, when Ahmed Shah conquered the district, and attached it to Western Afghanistan. In 1809, its governor, Mohammed Azad Khan, profiting by a change of dynasty, set up the standard of independence; and in 1816, he defeated an army which had been sent against him. In 1819, Runjeet Singh, sheik of Lahore, seized upon this territory, and annexed it to the Sikh dominions. On the overthrow of the Sikh monarchy by the British arms in 1846, C. was transferred, with Jummu, to the maharajahship of Gholab Singh. The power of Gholab Singh is wholly of his own creation. His ancestors were very anciently rajahs of the town and district of Jummu, which, after lapsing to the sovereign of Lahore, was adroitly recovered from Runjeet Singh, on conditions of feudal tenure, by its present possessor. In this and other acquisitions his independence was proclaimed and sanctioned by the British authorities; and we further made over to him the province of C. in sovereignty, saluting him by the royal title of maharajah. It remains to be seen whether our confidence in this adventurer has been well-placed Cashmere, the capital of the above province, called also Sirinagur, and sometimes Nagaz, is situated in N lat. 34° 7½, and E long. 75° 9½, 200 m. N of Lahore; at an alt. of about 5,500 ft. above sealevel. Rennel places it in N lat. 33° 49', E long. 73° 11'. This city has been supposed, but there is reason to believe incorrectly, to be the same as that which is mentioned by Herodotus under the name of Caspira or Caspiria. It extends on both sides of the Jelum for about the space of 3 m.; and the different parts of it thus disjoined are in some degree connected by four or five wooden bridges thrown across the river. The houses are slightly built of brick and mortar, with which is intermixed a large proportion of timber framing work. Many of them are two or three, and some even four or more stories high. The usual manner in which the different parts of these houses are appropriated is this: The lower floor is occupied by the cattle; the second, by the family; the third, and those above, are used as warehouses. On a standing roof of wood, there is laid a covering of fine earth, by means of which a defence is formed equally useful in every season. During winter, it shelters the buildings from the great quantity of snow that falls at that period, and communicates an agreeable warmth; in summer, it yields a refreshing coolness; and the tops of the houses being at the same time planted with a variety of flowers, exhibit at a distance the view of a beautifully chequered parterre. The environs of the town, in general, are laid out in private gardens, which skirting the banks of the Jelum, or supplied with canals from the dal Many of the houses in the city are large and commodious; no buildings, however, occur particularly worthy of remark. The Cashmerians, indeed, boast much of a wooden mosque, but its claim to distinction appears to be very moderate. The subadar, or governor, resided in a fortress, known by the name of Shereghur, occupying the SE quarter of the city, where most of his troops were also quartered. The streets of C. are commonly narrow and confined, and choked with the filth of the inhabitants, who are proverbially unclean. The pop. of this city appears to have dwindled from 200,000 to about 40,000 since the middle of last century. Above two-thirds are Mahommedans; the other third are Hindus of the Bramin, Chutri, and Bunya castes.
