Von Hugel's Kaschmir.—Masson's Journeys.—Asiatic Journal. — Asiatic Researches. CASHNA, CASHENAH, or CASSINA, a state in the interior of Africa, lying between Haussa and Bornu, and separated by the Niger from Meli. Little is known of it, except that it is a hilly and peculiarly fertile district, producing barley, millet, and the durra of the Arabs, in large quantities; and excellent senna, which forms an important article of traffic with Tripoli.—The cap., bearing the same name, is situated in about 13° 10' N lat., and 5° 50' E long., 5 days' journey to the N of the Niger. It is surrounded by walls, and is said to be a large city.—Its most northern province is Agadez, which appears to have recently been tributary to Timbuctu. Gaber, 5 or 6 days W by S of Cashna, is a neat walled town of considerable size, and the capital of a large and fertile district, enclosed between mountainranges of great height, and traversed by the Niger. The Fellatahs appear to have wrested C. from the king of Bornu. It is said that a portion of the native pop. are Christians, and wear large wooden crosses suspended from their necks. C. is said to be the most westerly country of Africa in which grapes are grown, or camels reared; but this, in regard to the last particular, is obviously a mistake, since that race cover the whole expanse of the Sahara as far as the Atlantic.—The commerce of C. with N Africa is maintained by a caravan which sets out from Fezzan, and, travelling by Assuda, Ganatt, and Agadez, arrives in upwards of sixty days at the capital: whence a journey of five days brings it to the Niger, which rolls along the S frontier of the empire. That river is described as here very broad, and rolling with such rapidity that no vessel can ascend its stream. It is said not to be generally used, either for the purposes of commerce or fishing; and caravans are conveyed over it on an ill-constructed raft. From that point they proceed through the countries to the south of the Niger, as far as the vicinity of the Gold coast. The currency of C., as over all Sudan, consists of those small shells called cowries, which are brought from the western coast, and 2,500 of which make about 10s. sterling. The exports to Barbary consist of gold dust, slaves, cotton cloths, and goat skins dyed red and yellow. The imports are different species of woollen stuffs, carpets, hardware, arms, knives, scissors, beads, mirrors, and toys. C. affords also a transit for all the trade carried on from Egypt and Eastern Barbary to the states S of the Niger.
CASHOBAS ISLANDS, a group of islands in the North Pacific, in the Caroline archipelago, in N lat. 9° 30', E long. 161°.

CASIBOS, a savage race inhabiting the banks of the Pachitea in Peru. They maintain incessant war with all the surrounding tribes; and recent communications from the Catholic mission- CAS aries at Ocopa represent them as untamed cannibals, devouring all the prisoners whom they capture in their warlike expeditions.
CASILDA (POINT), a headland of the island of Cuba, on the SW coast, in W long. 80° 30'.
CASILLAS-DE-FLORES, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 80 m. SW of Salamanca, 23 m. SSW of Ciudad-Rodrigo.

CASINA (CAYO), or St. George's Key, an islet off the coast of British Honduras, 71 m. NE of Belize, in N lat. 17° 33′ 5″, W long. 88° 4′ 54″. It was formerly the chief place of trade on this coast; and is still resorted to as a place of residence by the merchants of Belize during the hot season.
CASINO-MONTE. See Monte-Casino.
CASITA, a town of Upper California, 180 m. WSW of Santa Fe.
CASMA, a town and port of Peru, in the prov. and 120 m. SSE of Truxillo.
CASNIGO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 11 m. NE of Bergamo, on the l. bank of the Serio. Pop. 1,460.

CASOLA, a parish of Tuscany, in the comp. of Pisa, in the Val-di-Magra, 26 m. SW of Pontremoli. Pop. 273.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Naples, district and 3 m. E of Castellamare. Pop. 2,174. The environs afford excellent wine.

CASOLE, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 20 m. W of Sienna, and 33 m. SSW of Florence, on a ramification of the Tuscan Alps. Pop. 1,113. It is well-built, and contains a fine collegiate church of the 12th century, a fort, and several squares. Fairs are held annually, and are much frequented.
CASOLI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo- Citra, 12 m. SW of Lanciano. Pop. 5,858.
CASORATE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 12 m. NW of Pavia. Pop. 2,500.—The Milanese obtained a victory over the Germans at this place in 1356.

CASORIA, a town of Naples, cap. of a district of the same name, in the prov. and 8 m. NNE of Naples. Pop. 7,924. It contains 4 handsome churches, and is celebrated as the birthplace of Pierre Martino. The district of C. comprises 8 cant.

CASPE, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 60 m. SE of Saragossa, on the l. bank and near the junction of the Guadalupe with the Ebro. Pop. 7,401. It is of great antiquity; and contains a castle, a parish-church, 3 convents, an hospital, and several alms-houses. It possesses manufactories of cloth, hats, and soap, and distilleries of brandy; and has an active trade in wool. The environs afford oil and silk in great abundance. The congress in which Ferdinand of Castile was called to the throne of Aragon, after the death of Don Martin, was held here.

CASPIAN SEA, an immense mediterranean sea, or inland lake of salt water, on the confines of Europe and Asia, known to ancient geographers as the Mare Hyrcanum or Caspium. The Arabians of the Middle ages gave it different appellations; such as the Bahr-el-Khazar, or sea of Khazar or Khozars, -the sea of Djordian, the sea of Dilem, the sea of Ghilan, the sea of Tabaristan, the sea of Baku. The Slaves called it Ghvalinskoe-moré, after the Ghuhvalisses or Khvalisses, a tribe located at the mouth of the Wolga. The different Turcoman tribes which roam around its borders commonly call it Denghiz or Tenghiz, that is, 'the Sea;' or sometimes Ak-Denghiz, 'the White sea.' The Persians know it by the appellation Kolzoum; the Turks by that of Bahri-Ghouz, or Bahri-Ghaze. The Chinese call it the Si-hai or Western sea. With the Armenians it bears the name of Gasbits-dzov; and with the Georgians those of Kaspis-igva, or 'the Caspian sea,' and the sea of Daroubandi or Derbend. This great inland sea separates the dominions of Russia from those of Persia and the Independent Tartars. It is now well-known to extend from N to S, though, scarcely a century ago, it was represented as passing from E to W. It is situated between 36° 40' and 47° 20' N lat., and 46° and 54° E long. Its length from N to S is about 750 m. In its N part, on the parallel of 45° 30', it has a breadth of 430 m.; but its average breadth is little above 200 m. Opinion of the ancients respecting the C.] We find this sea noticed at a very early period by ancient writers Herodotus, who lived in the 5th cent., A. C., mentions it, and gives such a description of it as corresponds nearly to what we know of its present state, particularly adverting to the circumstance of its having no apparent outlet, nor any visible connection with any of the great seas. "The C.," says he, "is a sea by itself, unconnected with any other. Its length is as much as a vessel can sail with oars in 15 days; and its greatest breadth as much as it can sail in 8 days. It is bounded, he adds, on the W by the Caucasus, and on the E by an immense plain which extends beyond the reach of the eye." Aristotle describes it in a manner similar to this; and, with his usual precision, contends that it ought to be called a great lake not a sea. With these writers also concurs Diodorus Siculus. But other ancient writers, of later date, have expressed themselves with respect to this sea in such a manner as would indicate, on their part, a more imperfect knowledge of it. Strabo speaks of the C. as a bay that communicates with the great northern ocean, from which it issues at first by a narrow strait, and then expands into a sea extending in breadth 500 stadia. With him Pomponius Mela agrees, and describes the strait by which the C. is connected with the ocean, as considerable in length, and in breadth so narrow that it had the appearance of a river. Pliny gives a similar description of it. In the age of Justinian, this opinion concerning the communication of the C. with the ocean was still prevalent. Some early writers, among whom is Quintus Curtius, seem to have considered the C. to be connected with the Euxine. Arrian also, who, from his residence for some time in the prov. of Cappadocia, as governor, might certainly have obtained more accurate information, declares in one place, that the origin of the C. was still unknown; and expresses a doubt whether it was connected with the Euxine, or with the great Eastern ocean which surrounds India. Ptolemy maintains its communication with the Euxine, but by a subterranean passage: conceiving it to be impossible, without some supposition of this sort, to account for the flowing of so many rivers into it, while there is no appearance of any channel issuing from it, nor any perceptible swelling of its water, except in the spring, when it is obviously occasioned only by the melting of the snow. Indeed it has in all ages been found a problem of great difficulty to furnish any tolerable solution of the phenomena connected with this sea, both in respect to the circumstances just alluded to, and also as it appears in the character of a large sheet of water possessing the distinguishing properties of sea-water, but dissevered and insulated apparently from all connection with the ocean. The waters of the Volga, the Yaik, the Yemba, the Kur or Cyrus, the Araxes, the Bystraia, the Aksa, the Koisa, the Terek, with many other rivers generally of considerable, some of them of very great magnitude, flow into the C.: what, it is reasonable to inquire, becomes of all this water, which certainly cannot be evaporated by the sun alone, yet for which there is no other apparent means of escape? And how, moreover, is it that we meet here, in an inland country, and even in the midst of deserts, with a sea detached from all others, yet possessing the same properties, and yielding similar productions? The notion of a subterranean passage is one, which, amidst these difficulties, occurs as naturally, and which really seems to offer as plausible a solution of them, as any other. The existence of such a connection has accordingly been strenuously asserted, both in ancient and in modern times; and various arguments have been adduced in support of it. Two of these are as follows: The C., it has been observed, rises very high with a W wind; whereas the Euxine, on the contrary, rages most when the wind is at E. It has hence been inferred, that the E wind favours the exit of the waters of the C., and the W wind impedes it. It has also been alleged that there is a whirlpool in a particular part of this sea, by which all its superfluous water is discharged into the Euxine; and it has been asserted, that near to the mouth of the vortex in the Euxine, there is found a species of sea-weed which grows only on the shores of the C; that a sort of fish peculiar to the Euxine has been observed in the C.; and lastly, that in former days, a fish was taken in that sea with a golden ring about its tail, on which was this inscription, "Mithridates mihi dabat in urbe Sinope libertatem et hoc donum." As to the first of these arguments, it is to be observed, that the connection attempted to be traced between the character and effects of particular winds and a subterranean communication between the Euxine and the C., does not seem to rest upon any satisfactory grounds. In fact, it is known that all winds which bring with them humid vapours, are more stormy than those which proceed from arid regions; and this circumstance is plainly sufficient of itself to account for the greater agitation produced in the C. by winds coming towards it in the direction of the Euxine and the Palus Mæotis, than is caused by E winds, which, in their approach to it, pass over a great extent | none that draw above 15 ft. A few years ago, the waves of the of dry and even parched land. Of the alleged vortex or whirlpool, it is enough to say that we find no notice taken of it in the latest accounts which we have relative to the C. Sea-weed grows everywhere along the shores of this sea; but we know nothing of any particular species of this kind of plant which grows only there. We would likewise require more particular information as to the fishes that are said to be found only in the C. and the Euxine seas. The story, taken from Kircher's 'Mundus Subterraneus,' respecting the fish with the ring, is unworthy of notice.-It has likewise been conceived, that a concealed communication exists between the C. and the gulf of Persia; and in proof of this it has been alleged, that there is every year seen floating on the water in the vicinity of Ormus, a quantity of willow-leaves, of which kind of tree, a great abundance grows in the environs of the C., but none in the interior of Persia. The Tigris, however, it is known, receives a great number of rivers from Kurdistan and the frontiers of Media, in which regions this tree is common; it occurs also about Bagdad, and in the more southern parts of Persia.-The phenomena of the C. have likewise been ascribed to the quality of the bottom, which does not consist of slime but of a shell sand, the particles of which, touching only in few points, render it very porous. The whole shores nearly are formed of a similar substance, layer upon layer, to the depth of 3 fathoms. Through this sand, it has been alleged that the water is filtered, and falls into an abyss beneath. Ancient limits.] It has been observed, that the districts extending in different directions from the C. are sandy and saline, and present the same shells that are found in the bed of that sea, but which are, at the same time, of a different character from those met with in the beds of the rivers which formerly or now flow into the C. Sea-salt also occurs in the same tracts, in beds of great depth, and in such quantity as to prevent all vegetation except that of marine plants. Salt-marshes are another distinguishing feature in this quarter; and, generally, from the appearance of the surface here,—the small degree of its elevation,—and the nature of the substances which either form the constituent parts of the prevalent soil, or present themselves in a more occasional manner in or upon it,—there seems reason to infer that this sea at one period reached beyond the limits within which it is at present contained. The conclusion to which we are led by these appearances, coincides, at the same time, with the results of certain historical notices which have been transmitted to us, as well as with what we know of the determination of ancient geographers respecting the position and the boundaries of this sea. Thus Ptolemy, it is known, gave to it an extent of 231 degrees from W to E, and made it advance 3° more to the N than it does at present; and, in the Byzantine historians, we find mention made of an extensive marsh that, even in the 5th cent., occupied a large portion of the tract of ground which lies to the N of Caucasus, and between the C, on the one hand, and the Euxine on the other. It thus appears probable, that in remote times, long prior to the era of our most ancient historical details, the C. extended, in a NW direction, beyond the Manytsch and the Sarpa; and towards the N, over all the plains that are now watered by the Volga, the Yaik, and the Emba; that it was united to the lake of Aral; and that it covered a part of the plains of Great Tartary. These suppositions being admitted, it may be allowed, that, in the time of Ptolemy, the C. had an extent somewhat greater than it has at present, though certainly not so great as that which is assigned to it by this geographer; for it was then separated from the lake of Aral, and had long ceased to have any communication with the sea of Azof. With even the latter of these seas, however, as has been already remarked, some indications of its early junction are still to be found in ancient authors, who assign to the sea of Azof a greater extent than it now has, and take notice of marshes and lakes to the eastward of it, which have been gradually disappearing, but of which the traces are not even yet entirely lost, and of which no more plausible account can be given than that they are the remains of one great sheet of water which at some former period covered all this district of country. Professor Pallas considers the actual junction between the C. and the Euxine seas to have been formed by a strait, which communicated on each side with the anciently extended limits of those seas. "It is very probable," says he, "nay almost beyond doubt, that the low countries of Ulagann-Ternik, Alabuga, and Byeloe, are the old bed of the strait which, agreeably to the hypothesis advanced in the third part of my former journey, united the C. with that of Azof. Even at this day the C., when swelled by tempests, easily overflows the extensive low countries before mentioned. The sandhills which at present separate these low countries from the Manytsh, manifestly originate from the sand banks thrown up by the C., which are carried by the wind into the steppe; or, perhaps, they have been in some degree formed by the sand that remained in the strait itself, as they occupy only a small tract between the two vallies. These sands have also choked up the passage at the mouth of the large river Kuma, which formerly had a free current into the C., by the gulf of the same name." [Travels in 1793 and 1794, vol. i. p. 296; see also p. 302.] There can indeed be no doubt that this sea formerly covered a larger space than it now does. M. de Mouraviev asserts that he fully recognised the ancient shores of the C. betwixt the present coasts and the S extremity of the sea of Aral. Even at this moment the C. is still subsiding; and the decrease of its waters is the more remarkable within these few years, as vessels drawing 18 ft. water could lately navigate this sea, and now it will admit of C. washed the walls of Baku; at present they are at a considerable distance from it; and the consequence is, that the ships-ofwar of the Russian imperial navy are no longer stationed in the gulf of Baku, but in a tolerably convenient port of the isle of Sara, which is 10 m. distant from it. But what appears most extraordinary is, that this subsiding of the waters has uncovered first the top, and then the lower parts, of a vast caravanserai, which is situated in the sea, at the distance of about 2 versts from the coast. Thus it appears, that at some period, more or less remote, the C. was actually much lower towards the W coast than it is at the present moment when it is observed to be subsiding. In support of this singular fact, it may be added that, according to the unanimous tradition of the country, people formerly went along the shore of the C. from Lankhara to Salian; and that the road, now partly covered by the waters of the sea, is no longer passable. The fall of the waters has also left exposed some new islands. One of these is several versts in extent; the soil of it is firm; and it is probable that in a few years it will be inhabited by fishermen like all the other isles in the C. The Djanderia, or the southern and principal of the three mouths by which the Sir discharges its waters into the Aral, has been dry for a period of ten years; and the Kouvan-deria, forming the middle branch, has certainly considerably diminished within the last hundred years. The environs of the Aral lake abundantly demonstrate that its shores have been gradually narrowing, and that the shifting sands are insensibly gaining upon it. The same observations have been made upon the Baraba lake in Western Siberia. Form and extent.] Notwithstanding the early attention which this sea seems to have received, it does not appear that any of the ancient authors who have been named, or others who have left any statements relative to it, succeeded in estimating its true form and extent. In the ancient maps which illustrate the geography of Ptolemy, it is delineated, we have to W. In modern times, the first considerable information obtained in Europe concerning the true form of the Caspian, was furnished by Anthony Jenkinson, an English merchant, who, with a caravan from Russia, travelled along a considerable part of its coasts in the year 1558. The general accuracy of his description was confirmed by a survey made by order of Peter the Great, A. D. 1718, when it was reported to be in length about 1,100 versts from the mouth of the Ural to the coast of Mazanderan: its greatest width, from the mouth of the Terek to the extremity of the bay of Mertvoi-Kultuk, being 8°, or rather more than 700 versts; and the measurement in other situations more to the southward varying with the position. In one place it was found to be 6°, or somewhat more than 525 versts; in another it was 2° 35', or 225 versts. The circumf. of the sea, comprehending the great gulfs, but excluding the little sinuosities, is stated at 4,180 versts. Whatever may be concluded concerning the ancient limits of the C., it will hardly admit of doubt that the chain of mountains which branches from the W of the Ural to the N of Orenburg, and reaches to the Volga, must in all ages have restricted it towards the N. In an E direction, the elevated level or steppe which now presents itself between this sea and the Aral, may, some think, have taken its rise from the quantity of sand rolled down by the Gihon, the Sir, and other rivers that still flow into the latter sea. This high plain, called Usturt, extends under the 45th parallel, with a breadth of 160 m. It is so steep towards both seas, that it rises above the C. 639 ft., and its fall towards the Aral is not less, though that sea is 117 ft. above the level of the C. It sinks so insensibly, however, that there is nowhere to be observed upon it anything like a connected chain of hills. Its extreme headlands on the C. are the Aksakal, the Surak, the Kara-Ul, and the Karatau, which form so many eminences round the bight of Tuk-kara-su, a S branch of the Mertvoï-Kultuk. The elevation of the Usturt certainly precludes the idea of any river having ever flowed into the C. from the Aral, or generally from Central Asia, at least since this table-land was raised to its present elevation; but the same great upheaving which formed it, may have been attended with a contemporaneous depression of Western Asia, and particularly of the bed of the C., which is 101 ft. below the level of the Black sea, as well as 117 ft. below that of the Aral. [Eichwald.] Depth, &c.] The greatest ordinary depth of this sea is from 450 to 520 ft. Hanway in one place could not find the ground with a sounding line of 2,889 ft. The bottom is muddy, and in some places intermixed with shells. It is in general shallow along the coasts; vessels of any considerable size must anchor at a considerable distance from the W shores.—The water of the C., like that of all great bodies of water from which there is no outlet, is salt; and the salt dews and rains which are frequent in the neighbourhood seem to prove that the saline particles are evaporated along with the water. The waters of the C. have a bitterness totally different from that of the ocean: this bitterness is said to resemble the taste of the bile of animals, and has been attributed to the great quantities of naphtha which are found in some adjacent districts, and with which the waters, particularly after a storm from the N or NW, are strongly impregnated. Gmelin, who analyzed the waters of the sea, found that, besides common sea salt, they contained a considerable quantity of Glauber salt, the presence of which he attributed to the naphtha. Coasts.] We will now rapidly sketch the coastremarked, as if its greatest length extended from E | line of this sea, beginning at Astrakhan, and proceeding Salong the W coast. The coast is low, and thickly sprinkled with islands, to the gulf of Kuma, in 44° 15' N; it then rises a little to the mouth of the Terek, after which it again sinks and is covered with reeds as far as the gulf of Agrakhan, under 43° 45'. In this gulf we observe the isle of Tchetchen, on which the Tartars of Kizliar kill great numbers of seals. From Agrakhan-point to Derbend, in 42° 12', the coast is low and sandy. From the vicinity of Derbend a chain of mountains runs parallel to the coasts towards the mouth of the Samur. Directly opposite to Nizovaja, at the mouth of the Kudrab, there is pretty good anchorage. The straits of Apcheron are formed by the islands of Sviatoi, Jyloi, and the Lebejei. From this strait the shore gradually rises to the port of Baku, which offered until lately a pretty good harbour. From the S point of the gulf of Baku to Cape Vezir the coast is mountainous. Opposite to the latter promontory are some small isles called the Svinoi. The gulf of Salian stretches from this point to the mouths of the Kisil-agatch, or Aras. Excellent sturgeon-fisheries are established here. The coast now becomes low and woody, and after running along the meridian of 49°, takes a sudden sweep inwards, so as to form the gulf of Enzelli or Mudab, in the Persian prov. of Ghilan, whence it runs to the S of E to the mouth of the Sufid-Rud or Kizil-Ozen. The coast then sweeps in a circular direction from NW to SE, and rises into lofty mountains covered with forests. It here presents one very lofty volcanic summit, the Demávend, covered with perpetual snow. From Amol to Asterabad its direction is to the N of E. Near to Asterabad is a large bay which offers pretty good anchorage. From this bay the coast runs N. Cape Gumychtepe, or the Serebrenoibougor, to the N of the mouth of the Gourgheu, in 1782 formed a distinct island, which became united to the continent about the commencement of the present century. The gulf of Balkan, under 40° N, takes its name from the mountains, the extreme points of the Usturt, by which it is surrounded on the N and E; it is enclosed on the W by the isles of Ogourtsa or Aidak, Derwich, and Tcheleken or Naphtha, and by the little peninsula of Krasnovedsk. The hills around the Balkan rise steep and precipitous from $ the coast, and present at their summit porphyritic formations. About 30 leagues from the S point of this peninsula are the straits and gulf of Karaboghaz, called by the Turkomans Konli-deria. This gulf is little known, and has not been visited by mariners. At the distance of 75 leagues from these straits, we observe, after passing the deep gulf of Kenderlinof, the great bay of Alexander; and 38 leagues to the NNW of the latter place is the projecting promontory of Cape Tuk-Karagan, a little to the N of which is the isle of Koulat. About 15 leagues N from Tuk-Karagan is a little tongue of land called Manghislak. The depth of the coast here is seldom so little as 6 fath., and generally between 10 and 18 fath. From Manghislak the coast runs E, and then takes a sudden turn to the N towards Cape Burunshuk. The next gulf which presents itself in this the C. quarter is the great Mertvoi-Kultuk or Dead gulf, in which several islands are situated. From the N horn of this bay, nearly on the 46th parallel, the coast sweeps round in a semicircular form to the mouths of the Ural; whence the line of low sandy marshy coast runs to the S of W to the mouths of the Volga. All the N coast of the C., from the mouth of the Yemba to the Volga, is low, and covered with reeds and sand-banks. Navigation.] In consequence of the manner in which the C. is enclosed on all sides by land, and its coasts being so much in the vicinity of high mountains, its navigation is, in some measure, necessarily of a peculiar kind. Certain winds domineer over it with such absolute sway that vessels are often deprived of every resource; and there is hardly one port or bay on its coasts that can be called perfectly safe. -There are no tides in the C., but strong currents prevail in it, which, uniting their influence with that of the violent storms of wind so frequent here, and common in some measure to all inland seas, occasion not a little danger to the description of vessels which principally navigate it. These difficulties are farther increased by the incredible numbers of shoals and small islands which this sea contains. The vessels which frequent the C. are commonly of rude construction, drawing from 9 to 10 ft. of water, and impelled by means of a single square sail, with the addition sometimes of top-sails. The long flat-bottomed craft, which among the Russians has now taken place of the circular kind of vessels that at an early period navigated this sea, are more remarkable for a clumsy strength than for any ingenuity in the mode of their formation. The vessels fitted out by the Persians are commonly made of elm, which abounds in their provinces southward of the C.; their sails are of cotton, the cables of flax, and some of the bark of trees. They have others of a somewhat different structure, and larger dimensions, of the burden of from 30 to 40 tons; but these are seldom able to keep the sea for more than 4 or 5 years. The most really serviceable, and indeed the most esteemed part of the Persian marine in this sea, seems to consist in the small boats called kirjiems. On the banks of the Volga there are large tracts of forestground, which supply a sufficiency of oak and other timber for the construction of vessels engaged in the commerce of the C. Good timber for the same purpose is also found in Ghilan and the adjacent provinces subject to Persia; and iron ore might be obtained from Mazanderan. The country all round this sea, where not in the state of a desert, is fertile in grain, fruits, and other productions. The water in the vicinity of the C. is frequently brackish; but here and there along its coasts, or in the islands which rise out from it, vessels may be supplied with water better fitted for use.—Salmon, sturgeon, and other fish abound in all parts of the C., as well as in the rivers which communicate with it. The salmon is as good as that of Riga and Archangel. The sterlet, various species of seals, diversified only or chiefly by their colour, and porpoises, occur in the C. The only shell-fish are three or four species of cockle, the common mussel, some species of snails, and one or two others.—This sea is much frequented by birds of various kinds: those most commonly seen in its vicinity are storks, herons, bitterns, spoonbills, red geese, and red ducks. The most beautiful of these is the red goose, which, however contrary to what is suggested by the name, is for the greater part white, having only the tips of the wings, the orbits of the eyes, the beak, and the feet of a scarlet colour. It is about the size of a stork, with a long neck and legs. The grebe, the crested diver, the pelican, the cormorant, and almost every species of gull frequent Commerce.] Alexander the Great, a short time before his death, gave directions to fit out a squadron in this sea, in order to survey it, and to discover whether it was connected either with the Euxine or with the Indian ocean. Seleucus Nicator, also, the first and most sagacious of the Syrian kings, entertained thoughts of forming a junction between the C. and the Euxine by a canal; and many ages afterwards, under the auspices of Peter the Great of Russia, a canal was made at Vishni-Volotshok, by means of which a passage may be made from the C. into the Volga; thence, in conjunction with some rivers and lakes, into the Neva; and so into the Baltic. The Persians had no intercourse by sea with India, but the valuable productions and elegant manufactures of that country were conveyed to all parts of their dominions by land-carriage; and those Indian commodities destined for the supply of the northern provinces were transported on camels, from the banks of the indus to those of the Oxus, and down that stream to the C. In more modern times, the Venetians and Genoese, as early as the 14th cent., had begun to transport, by way of this sea, the Indian, Persian, and Arabian commodities with which they supplied the southern parts of Europe, through Astrakhan, to their magazines at Azof and Caffa. From Astrakhan the goods were conveyed up the Volga, then by land to the Don, and afterwards forwarded down that river to Azof. The N part of the European continent was, during the same period, supplied with Asiatic goods, which were sent by the Rus sian traders from Astrakhan to their principal magazine at Wisby, a Hanseatic town in the isle of Gothland. Towards the end of the cent., in consequence of the devastations produced by the wars of Timur, a transfer took place of this trade from Astrakhan to Smyrna and Aleppo; and the Arabian commerce—for which, in fact, these places lay more convenient—never till recent times returned thither, though a part of the Persian traffic was afterwards restored to its old channel. While the provinces of Kasan and Astrakhan remained under the government of the Tartars, the camp of the khan was a mart for the Russian and Persian merchants; and as the site of their camp was often changed, Astrakhan and Terek became at length two principal places of resort. This commerce, frequently obstructed and interrupted by numerous banditti, was however precarious, until the conquest of Kasan and Astrakhan by the Russians opened a ready communication between Moscow and the C.; and Ivan Vasilivitch II. having garrisoned Astrakhan with troops, rendered it the chief emporium of the eastern trade. Soon after their first intercourse with Archangel, the English obtained the czar's permission to pass through his dominions into Persia, and to carry on an exclusive trade over the C. In 1558, Jenkinson, the first Englishman who navigated that sea, landed at Mangishlak, upon the E shore, passed by land, through the country of the Turkoman Tartars, to Bokhara, the capital of Great Bucharia, and returned to Moscow in the following year. In 1561 he again sailed over the C., and proceeding to the coast of Shirvan, went by land to Casbin, and obtained from the sophi permission to trade into Persia. Similar expeditions were set on foot by other merchants. This traffic was chiefly confined to the ports of Tumen, Derbend, Baku, and the coast of Ghilan; but having been much impeded by the banditti that frequent the shores of the C., as well as by the wars which then prevailed between the Turks and the Persians, no English vessel for more than a century ever appeared upon that sea, and the trade may be considered to have been altogether extinct, till at length Peter I. having subdued the northern provinces of Persia, endeavoured to induce the English to engage anew in this commerce. The many unsuccessful attempts, however, that had been made since the 16th cent., and in which large sums had been lost, deterred our countrymen from entering into the views of that monarch; and so little appeared to be the advantage of possessing territories which at the time of their acquisition seemed to promise very beneficial results, that, in consideration of obtaining the liberty of import and export duty free in all the harbours of the C., with some other minor advantages, the empress Anne agreed to relinquish these provinces to Nadir Shah. Captain Elton, an Englishman in the Russian service, first renewed the project of a British trade over the C. Influenced by his persuasion, the British merchants of St. Petersburg turned their attention to this commerce. A vessel, specially built for the navigation of this sea, was loaded with the goods considered to be the best adapted for the Persian market, and a factory was established at Reshd, in the province of Ghilan. Eventually, in consequence as well of disputes among the English themselves, as between them and the Russians, Elton entered into the service of Nadir Shah, and assisted in constructing vessels, by means of which the Persian court seems to have entertained the idea of establishing their own supremacy on this sea. This proceeding reasonably gave umbrage to the court of St. Petersburg, and Elizabeth, in 1746, withdrew her permission to the English merchants of passing through her dominions in the pursuit of the C. commerce. On the death of Nadir Shah, who had for some time permitted the English to trade to Persia, their factory was pillaged, and their commerce annihilated. Elton, after various vicissitudes of fortune, by a violent and premature death paid the forfeit either of his imprudence or of his crimes. Under Alexis Michaelovitsh, Astrakhan became the centre of the Russian trade with Persia; and to this place there resorted merchants from Bucharia, Crim-Tartary, Persia, and even India. The vessels of the Russians, as has been already remarked, being rudely framed, without decks, and consequently exposed to frequent shipwrecks, the czar drew from Amsterdam several ship-builders, for the purpose of constructing others more calculated to weather the storms of the C. His designs of improvement, however, were frustrated by the rebellion of the Cossacks of the Don under Stenko Razin, and the devastations occasioned by it. When this revolt was quelled, the greater part of the commerce of the C. fell into the hands of the Armenian merchants established in Astrakhan, who settled factories in both the Russian and Persian territories. During this period, neither the Russian nor the Armenian traders had penetrated farther than Nizabad, and their chief settlement was at Shamachie, till the year 1711, when that town having been taken by the Lesghis, the factory was destroyed. In 1721, Peter L having obtained, as has been stated, the cession of the provinces of Daghistan, Shirvan, Ghilan, and Mazanderan, the C. commerce was renewed. He established a Russian mercantile company trading to the C., with a capital of 400 achins, or shares, of the value of 150 rubles, or £30 each. The chief factories of this company were formed at Astrakhan and Kislar; and when the right of property in those distant provinces was resigned in manner afore-mentioned, the Russian merchants, still retaining their exclusive privilege, obtained the liberty of trading without payment of any duty to all the havens of the C., and were allowed to build houses and magazines, subject to the single condition of obedience to the laws of the country. The benefit of the monopoly, with the other advantages enjoyed by this company, were confirmed to them by Anne and Elizabeth. Yet the commerce continued inconsiderable, till, in 1762, Catharine II. abolished their exclusive privileges, and permitted all her subjects without exception, to take part in the Persian trade. By degrees, the Russian trade on the C. rose to considerable consequence; it has, however, been a good deal injured by means of a contraband trade carried on in Shamachie, and other inland Persian towns, by the Armenian merchants, who, from their knowledge of the country and language, have it in their power to undersell the Russians in the parts which they severally frequent. The following is a general view of the state of this trade for several considerably distant years in the latter part of the last century. In 1760, the amount of the exports was £36,100, of the imports £42,100, in all £78,200; with a balance against Russia of £6,000. In 1768, the exports were £87,700, the imports £63,700; the amount of the whole, £151,400. Balance in favour of Russia, £4,000. In 1775, the sum of the exports was £125,400, of the imports £64,120; giving a total amount of £189,520, to which is to be added the farther sum of £10,000 for the traffic of Georgia and the nations of the Caucasus, making the whole sum £199,520; the balance, in respect to the part of this sum stated under the distinct heads of exports and imports, being in favour of Russia £61,280. In these statements, it is to be understood, that no account is made of the contraband trade, the amount of which was considerable, The Russian trade of the C. is not only carried on with Persia, but also with all the independent Tartarian tribes to the E and SE; with Kashgir and Yarkand in the Lesser Bucharia; and even with the W part of Tibet, and the vale of Cashmere. Russian caravans of 500 or 600 persons have even come on horseback to the fair of Ghertokh, or Ghertope, and by supplying the Tibetians with coral beads, cut up the trade in that article from Delhi and Benares. The chief marts of this commerce are Astrakhan, Orenburg, Gurief, Terki, Derbend, Tarku, Baku, and Salian. Astrakhan is the chief seat of the Persian commerce, there being above 5,000 resident merchants there, many of whom have ships on the C., and export to Persia woollens, furs, iron, steel, lead, linen, and other Russian products; procuring, in return, silk from Ghilan and Samarcand; cotton from Mazanderan and Ispahan, also drugs, tapestry, gold, pearls, and diamonds, which articles are also imported by Orenburg and Kasan. Great quantities of raw cotton are brought to Orenburg by the Tartars of Khiva, who also bring gold and gems to Astrakhan. The Tartar havens to which the Russian merchants resort, are those of Mangishlak, and the ports in the bay of Balkan. From an idea that the gold and gems brought by the Khivensian merchants to Astrakhan were the natural produce of the country, the Russians attempted a settlement in 1720, on the E of the C., in order to pave the way for the conquest of Khiva; but the Tartars suspecting the design at the very first, rose upon the Russian forces, and cut them off. A great trade is carried on at Orenburg with the Kirghisian Tar- CAS 358 tars, in cattle and horses Another equally great annual fair is held at Omsk, whither articles of the same kind, and to an equal amount, are brought by the middle Kirghisian horde. In return for these, the Russians supply these hordes with cloths, furniture, and other manufactured goods, but are prohibited from supplying them with arms, ammunition, and other warlike stores and implements, as they are very troublesome and restless.—The number of vessels which entered the Russian ports on the C. in 1845 was 121; in 1846, 116; in 1847, 110; and in 1848, 161.
CASPUENAS, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Guadalaxara, partido of Brihuega, 12 m. ENE of Guadalaxara, on an affluent of the Tajuna.

CASS, a county in the NW of the state of Georgia, U. S., watered by the Etowah and Sulacoe. Its surface is hilly. Its cap. is Cassville, 144 m. NW of Milledgeville. Pop. 9,390.—Also a co. in the SW part of Michigan, watered by the St. Joseph, Christiana, and Dowagiake, rivers. Area 528 sq. m. Pop. 5,710. Its cap. is Cassopolis, 167 m. SW of Detroit. -Also a co. in the N of the state of Iowa, watered by the Wabash and the Eel. Area 415 sq. m. Pop. 5,480. Its cap. is Logansfort.—Also a co. in Illinois, watered by branches of Sangamon river. Area 256 sq. m. Pop. 2,981. Its cap. is Virginia.—Also a township in Hancock co., in the state of Ohio, 100 m. NW of Columbus. Pop. 617.—Also a township in Ionia co., in Michigan, 133 m. WNW of Detroit. Pop. 202.—Also a river of Michigan, a branch of the Saginaw, which it enters at about 20 m. from its mouth.

CASS LAKE, an expansion of the Upper Mississippi, in Michigan, 50 m. W of Lake Winnipec. It has a surface of about 120 sq. m. It is 1,330 ft. above sea-level; and 160 ft. below the level of Itasca lake, or Lac la Biche, in which the W fork of the Mississippi takes its rise, 182 m. SW of C. lake.
CASSABA. See KASSABA.
CASSAGNABE'RE, or Cassaignebe're, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Garonne, cant. of Aurignac, 9 m. N of Saint-Gaudens. Pop. 1,413.
CASSAGNAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lozère, cant. of Barre, 8 m. ESE of Florac. Pop. 770. Antimony is mined here.

CASSAGNES-BE'GONHE'S, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Rodez.—The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,248.—The com. and town, 12 m. S of Rodez, on the Scor, has a pop. of 1,545.
CASSAGNES-COMTAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Rignac. Pop. 1,287.

CASSANDRA, or KASANDERAH, a peninsular district of Turkey, in Romelia, in the sanjak of Salonica, running out between the gulf of Salonica on the W, and that of C. on the E. There is a small village of the same name near its SW extremity. Its extreme point on the SE is Cape Palliuri, between which and Cape Drepano, lies the entrance to the gulf of C., the ancient Sinus Toronaicus, which has a length of 20 m. in a NW and SE direction, with a medium breadth of about 7 m.
CASSANDRIA. See CADSANT.

CASSANGE, a territory in the interior of Africa, to the E of Benguela, intersected by the parallel of 10° S, and the meridian of 20° E. Little is known of it, save that it is inhabited by nomade tribes of warlike habits, who have occasionally threatened the Portuguese settlements.

CASSANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 8 m. ESE of Castrovillari. The buildings composing it are large and well-constructed, and rise like a theatre in the concave recess of a steep mountain. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Reggio; and contains a cathedral, 4 convents, an episcopal seminary, a custom-house, and the ruins of an ancient castle. Pop. 4,000. Its manufactures are maccaroni, leather, linen, and silk stuffs. Cotton, silk, fruits, and soda, are produced in the environs.—Also a town of Naples in Principato-Ultra, in the district and 9 m. SW of San- Angelo-Lombardi, on a hill rising above the Calore. Pop. 4,629.—Also a town of Naples, in the Terra-di- Bari, 14 m. NE of Altamura. Pop. 4,713.

CASSANO-D'ADDA, a town of Venetian-Lombardy, in the gov. and deleg. of Milan, 6 m. E of Gorgonzora, on the r. bank of the Adda. Pop. 2,000. There are silk-works here.—On the 16th of August, 1705, an important battle was fought here between the French under Vendome, and the Imperialists under Prince Eugene. And on the 25th of April, 1799, the Russian general Suvaroff here defeated the French under Moreau.
CASSANO-MAGNANO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 24 m. NW of Milan. Pop. 1,900.
CASSANO-SPINOLA, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 9 m. S of Tortona, on the Scrivia. Pop. 1,550.
CASSARO, a town of Sicily, in the district and 15 m. NNW of Noto, on the Anapo. Pop. 1,680.
CASSAY. See MUNIPORE.

CASSEL, a town of Germany, the cap. of Hesse- Cassel, on the Fulda, 84 m. NE of Coblentz, 72 m. S by W of Hanover, in N lat. 51° 19′ 20″, E long. 9° 35' 18". The old town and upper new town are on the l. or W bank of the Fulda; the lower new town is on the E bank. The elector's palace, the museum, and the public library of 80,000 vols., are in the upper new town; the castle is in the lower new town. The two divisions of the town are connected by a stone bridge, 273 ft. in length. During the seven years' war, this town was fortified; but its ramparts were demolished in 1764. Its trade is not of great importance; but it possesses manufactories of cotton, silk, and woollen fabrics, leather, carpets, snuff, porcelain, chemical dyes, soap, starch, hardware, and musical instruments. The pop. is about 30,000. CASSEL, CASTEL, or KASSEL, a town in the duchy of Hessen, on the r. bank of the Rhine, opposite Mentz, with which it is connected by a bridge of boats measuring 766 paces in length. Pop. 2,500. A large cattle-market is annually held here.

CASSEL, a canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. of Hazebrouck. - The cant. comprises 13 com.—The town commands a most extensive view over the flat and fertile plains of Flanders, being situated on an isolated hill which attains an alt. of 544 ft., 7 m. NNW of Hazebrouck. Pop. 4,410 in 1841. It has manufactories of lace, linen, soap, and earthenware.—This town occupies the site of the Castellum Morinorum of the Romans. It was at one time strongly fortified.
CASSEL. See HESSE-CASSEL.
CASSELHAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, comarca of Setubal, nearly opposite Lisbon. It has a small port on the l. bank of the Tagus.
CASSEMABAD, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak-Ajemi, 18 m. S of Kum.

CASSEN, or Kassan, a village on the l. bank of the Tomat, in the district of Kamamyl, or Quamamyl, in Nubia, to the N of Abqoulgui, in about 10° 50' N lat. The surrounding district is rich in auriferous sands; and it appears that the search for gold has been recently resumed here by the pasha of Egypt, with considerable success. A Russian engineer commenced his researches here in March 1849, on the E side of the Tomat. "In about an hour's journey from the river the engineer came to a place encompassed by small hillocks not higher than 40 or 50 feet. He directed that they should be dug into about half their height, and the excavated materials carefully examined. He continued these operations for six or seven days, the Siberian workmen washing the sands I range of Syria, in the pash of Aleppo, between the sea $ CAS upon a drum. On the eighth day he concluded from the results that these sands were richer than those in many parts of Siberia; for 100 poods of sand in Siberia produced but 25 habbas, whilst 100 poods were yielding at Cassen from 50 to 52 habbas. On the ninth day, he directed his Russian workmen to prepare the machines for washing, whilst he departed to make new researches with about 1,000 Egyptian soldiers, using them occasionally for the works and sometimes for personal safety. He made several experiments on the banks of the rivers Ramla, Dys, Gucka, and Benishangol, and afterwards at Sorgonti and Gamamil, about eight hours' journey S of C. Here he found the sands considerably richer than those of C. In his travels he met with quantities of argillaceous iron, rock-crystal, and zinc, but no other metals, nor any vestige or appearance of coal. On taking his departure from C. he left the works under the direction of two Arab mineralogists who had studied in Germany and Siberia."—Bengal Journal.
CASSENEUIL, a town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Cancon, 5 m. NW of Villeneuve-sur-Lot, on the r. bank of the Lot. Pop. 1,964.
CASSERES, or Casserras, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 48 m. W of Taragona, on the r. bank of the Algas.—Also a town of Spain, in the prov. and 48 m. NNW of Barcelona.
CASSIMAQUAGAN, a river of Lower Canada, an affluent of the Matapediac.
CASSINA. See Cashna.
CASSINE, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 10 m. S of Alexandria, on the l. bank of the Bormida. Pop. 3,500.
CASSINGTON, a parish in Oxfordshire, 5 m. NW of Oxford. Area 2,990 acres. Pop. 381.

CASSIQUIARI, a large and copious branch of the Rio Negro, in South America, remarkable for forming, between the parallels of 2° and 3° N lat., a navigable connexion between the two great rivers, the Orinoco and the Amazon. This communication, though constantly asserted by the Jesuit missionaries, was denied by others, and its existence generally questioned, until the fact was ascertained beyond a doubt, by Humboldt, who, entering the sea from the Rio Negro, ascended its stream until he entered that of the Orinoco. The communication of the Rio Negro with the C., and that of the C. with the river Orinoco, was thus settled beyond the possibility of dispute; and the Rio Negro being a branch of the Amazon, the junction likewise of all these rivers by the natural canal of the C., which branches off from the Rio Negro-here called by the natives the Guainia—in a NNE direction, at the point where that river changes its course from E to S. An uninterrupted navigation of 381 leagues, says Humboldt, conducted me from the Rio Negro into the Orinoco, or from the frontiers of Brazil to the coast of Caracas. The length of the C. is 120 m. direct distance, or 176 m. including its windings; and where it strikes off from the Orinoco, its width is 100 yards. It widens considerably on approaching its junction with the Guainia; and at its mouth is 550 yards, the Guainia being about 600 yards in width.

CASSIS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, arrond. and 9 m. SE of Marseilles, cant. of La Ciotat, at the mouth of a narrow valley opening upon the Mediterranean. Pop. 2,093. It has a small port, from which 81,446 quintals of merchandise, and 79,845 q. of building materials, were exported in 1840. It exports oil, fruit, and wine. Between C. and La Ciotat, coral is fished.—The abbé Bartholemy was born here. CASSIUS, Casius, or Jabel - Akrah, a coast and the l. bank of the Orontes, which has its mouth in the bay of Antioch, 4 m. N by W & W from the foot of C. It attains an alt. of 5,318 ft. above sealevel. Its declivities towards the sea are precipitous. With the Jabel-el-Anzeyry it unites and forms a broad table-land, and may be regarded as continuous with the Lebanon or Libanus. It is composed, from its base in the sea to its conical summit, of indurated limestones.
CASSNEU, a village of Norway, in the island of Andorgöe, Finmark, at the foot of the Foxfield which has an alt. of 666 toises above sea-level.

CASSOOR, or Kassur, a town in the Punjab of Northern India, 7 m. from the Sutledge, on the road from Ferozepur to Lahore. It was formerly an extensive place; and has still an active trade, particularly in saddlery.
CASSOP, a township in Kelloe p., Durham. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 1,076.
CASSOPO, a small town and port on the NE coast of the island of Corfu, near the site of the ancient Cassiope.
CASSOPOLIS, a town in Cass co., in the state of Michigan, on the NW border of Stone lake. Pop. 400.
CAST, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. and 3 m. SW of Chateaulin. Pop. 1,711.
CAST (SAINT), a commune and small port of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Matignon. Pop. 1,481.
CASTAGNA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra 1ma. 15 m. NW of Civita-di-Penne. on the r. bank of the Maone. Pop. 1,225.

CASTAGNERO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 30 m. SE of Verona, and 6 m. SE of Legnago, on the r. bank of the Adige, at the point where the canal of C. branches off from the Adige to the Canal Bianco. Pop. 1,800.
CASTAGNETO-DELLA-GHERARDESCA, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 50 m. S of Pisa. Pop. 1,860.
CASTAGNETTO, a village of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 10 m. NE of Turin, near the r. bank of the Po. Pop. 1,200.
CASTALLA, a town of Spain, in Valencia, in the prov. and 15 m. NW of Alicant, on a small river of the same name. Pop. 3,000.
CASTAMOUNI. See KASTAMUNI.
CASTANARES, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 4 m. E of Burgos, on the r. bank of the Arlançon.
CASTANDET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Grenade-sur-l'Adour, 10 m. SE of Mont-de-Marson. Pop. 1,060.
CASTANESA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 60 m. ENE of Huesca, on the l. bank of the Baliera.

CASTANET, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, arrond. of Toulouse.—The cant. comprises 16 com.—The town, 6 m. SSE of Toulouse, near the Canal du Midi, has a pop. of 1,060.
CASTANHEIRA, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 20 m. NE of Lisbon, near the r. bank of the Tagus. Pop. 1,000.
CASTANHEIRO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, on the r. bank of the Tavora, in the comarca of Trancoso, 12 m. SE of Lamego. Pop. 2,000.
CASTANHEIRO-DO-VOUGA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca of Feira, 9 m. E of Aveiro, at the confluence of the Agueda and Conto. Pop. 700.
CASTANO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and deleg. of Milan, 4 m. NNW of Cuggiono-Maggiore. Pop. 2,500. CAS
CASTASEGNA, or Castelsegno, a parish and town of Switzerland, in the Grisons, at the extremity of the Val Bregaglia, at an alt. of 2,450 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 200.
